text: Three 8.5mm robotic trocars are positioned in the peri-umbilical region, right lateral mid-abdomen, and left lateral mid-abdomen. The sigmoid colon is visibly extending into the hernia but is easily reduced. An adhesion between the sigmoid colon and the hernia defect is observed and left intact. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible running lateral to the hernia.

The surgeon initiates the creation of a subperitoneal flap at the medial umbilical ligament, extending it inferiorly and across the left rectus muscle. The flap is carefully raised, ensuring some areolar tissue remains on it to maintain robustness for closure over the mesh later.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the hernia defect, carefully preserving the visible inferior epigastric vessels. The dissection is carried out both laterally and medially to expose the hernia defect with a wide margin, ensuring ample space for mesh overlap.
The surgeon dissects down to the origin of the inferior epigastric vessels from the femoral vessels, then reduces the hernia by pulling the associated fat out of the femoral hernia defect.
The surgeon dissects anterior to the femoral vessels, ensuring the area is clear for the mesh to sit flat against the retroperitoneum, avoiding any fat that could interfere with the mesh placement.

The surgeon dissects the fat along Cooper's ligament to ensure the mesh can be placed directly on musculoskeletal structures, preventing it from floating into the hernia defect. Fat overlying the femoral vessels is carefully dissected back to allow the mesh to sit securely inferiorly, avoiding potential hernia formation posterior and inferior to the mesh.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to ensure the mesh overlaps muscle and tendon, exposing the iliopubic tract medially.

The dissection is completed, revealing the iliopubic tract superiorly, Cooper's ligament inferiorly, and the femoral vessels and inferior epigastric vessels laterally. The surgeon unfolds the progrip mesh, marked at its center, and positions it over the center of the hernia defect.

The surgeon gently manipulates the progrip mesh into place, ensuring it adheres to musculoskeletal structures rather than surrounding fat. Fat is carefully pulled away from the mesh to prevent it from floating into the hernia defect.

The surgeon positions the progrip mesh medially and laterally, ensuring it sits flat against the musculoskeletal structures without curling, and then unfolds the mesh completely over the hernia defect.

The surgeon places the mesh underneath the previously dissected fat, ensuring it does not float into the hernia defect. The mesh is unfolded first inferiorly, then superiorly, and finally completely unfolded to cover the defect adequately.

The surgeon unfolds the mesh completely and presses its sides and corners down to ensure it sits flat against the pelvic contour, preventing it from rolling. As the mesh is pressed into position, it adheres more firmly, making further adjustments increasingly difficult.

The surgeon ensures the mesh is flat and covers the hernia defect with a wide margin. The flap is closed using an absorbable V-lock suture, completely extraperitonealizing the mesh to prevent adhesions. Any holes in the flap are also closed with the V-lock suture.
The surgeon completes the running of the V-lock suture, ensuring the suture is locked in place by backing up on itself to secure the closure of the flap over the mesh.
